planting.note: Plant after all frost danger has passed in partially shaded spots; New Guinea impatiens is propagated primarily by cuttings and sold as bedding transplants, not typically started from seed.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in partial shade with some direct morning sun; New Guinea impatiens is distinct from the common impatiens (I. walleriana) in its tolerance of sun and heat, larger flowers, and bolder tropical foliage with prominent leaf variegation in many cultivars. Unlike I. walleriana, it resists downy mildew, which devastated common impatiens in many regions. The 2–3 inch flowers in red, pink, orange, white, salmon, and lavender bloom continuously from spring through frost. Keep soil evenly moist — plants wilt dramatically when dry but recover quickly with watering. Pinch growing tips when planting to encourage branching. Excellent in containers, window boxes, and hanging baskets in bright shade.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or plugs in spring; golden creeping jenny spreads rapidly by surface runners and is particularly effective cascading over container edges or covering shaded slopes.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in partial shade; the golden-leafed cultivar 'Aurea' is one of the most valuable groundcovers for illuminating shaded areas where few plants thrive. The vivid chartreuse-gold rounded leaves create a brilliant carpet that contrasts beautifully with dark green hostas, ferns, and astilbes. Unlike the green species, the gold form needs some shade in hot climates to prevent leaf scorch, but it fades to pale yellow-green in deep shade — dappled light produces the most intense gold color. Small yellow flowers in early summer. Spreads by horizontal stems that root at nodes; divide and replant crowded mats every 2 years.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall, 4–6 inches apart, in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; drumstick allium blooms in early summer with spring-planted bulbs or mid-summer with fall-planted ones.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun in fall; drumstick allium is one of the latest-blooming ornamental alliums, producing distinctive egg-shaped flowerheads 1–1.5 inches in diameter on slender wiry stems in June and July. What makes it particularly striking is the two-tone effect — the florets open from bottom to top, with the lower portions turning deep burgundy-purple while the upper ones remain green, creating a jewel-like gradient. The dried seed heads persist and remain ornamental through winter. Excellent for cutting and drying. The small bulbs multiply readily to form clumps. Naturalizes over time and requires virtually no care once established in well-drained soil.
sources: Brent and Becky's Bulbs cultural guide

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in water or moist perlite year-round; in zones 10–11 grow as an outdoor perennial shrub; in colder zones grow as a houseplant or move outdoors to shaded spots in summer.

directions: Root stem cuttings with at least two nodes in water or moist perlite — they root reliably within 2–3 weeks. Angel wing begonias are cane-stemmed begonias grown for their large, wing-shaped leaves (often spotted white on dark green surfaces) and pendulous clusters of coral-red to pink flowers that appear repeatedly from spring through fall. Unlike tuberous or wax begonias, they grow as shrubby upright plants 2–4 feet tall and are true perennials in frost-free climates. They prefer bright indirect light and well-drained potting mix kept evenly moist. Repot when roots fill the container; pinch tips to control height and encourage bushiness. Remove spent flower clusters to maintain a tidy appearance.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep and 6–8 inches apart in well-drained soil in fall; Persian allium bulbs naturalize well in sunny, dry spots and increase year over year.

directions: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep in well-drained to dry soil in full sun in fall; Persian allium is one of the most popular large-flowered ornamental alliums, producing perfectly spherical 4–5 inch flower balls of hundreds of tiny star-shaped purple flowers on tall stiff stems in late spring. The cultivar 'Purple Sensation' is among the deepest purple-flowering bulbs available. Foliage appears in early spring and often dies back by bloom time, so plant alliums among hostas, daylilies, or other perennials that will fill in around the fading leaves. Alliums are strongly deer-resistant. After blooming, the spherical seed heads dry to silvery brown and remain ornamental through summer and fall.
sources: Brent and Becky's Bulbs cultural guide
